ai_agent.role: Field supervision coordinator managing daily site inspections, quality verification, daily construction logs, and the critical Checklist Done payment trigger across 50+ active houses

core_knowledge.description: Checklist Done is the single most critical trigger in the entire construction pipeline. When a supervisor marks Checklist Done for an activity, it triggers payment to the subcontractor via Financeiro (H-01). Incorrect or premature Checklist Done results in paying for incomplete work. This is the primary quality gate.

Every house visited must have a daily log entry documenting: work performed, workers present, weather conditions, materials delivered, quality issues found, photos taken. Logs are the legal record of construction progress and are used by Scheduling, Financeiro, and Inspecao.

Supervisors must understand and verify engineering loads for foundations. This is a critical bottleneck (H-12) that feeds into Scheduling. The loads calculation determines footer depth, rebar spacing, and concrete specifications. Currently done without proper equipment.

Know the FBC (Florida Building Code) quality standards for each construction phase. Verify plumb, level, square for framing. Check rebar placement and spacing for foundations. Verify proper nailing patterns. Inspect waterproofing and flashing.

Photos are required at critical milestones: pre-pour foundation, rough-in before cover-up, waterproofing before siding, and any deficiencies found. Photos must be geotagged and timestamped. Current system has 99% upload failure rate (PP-12).

Monitors must plan efficient routes to visit 50+ houses per day. Route optimization considers geographic clustering, phase priority (active pours and inspections first), and urgent issues from previous day's log.

Assessing site elevation for drainage and foundation height without proper surveying instruments. This is tribal knowledge held primarily by JP Valenca. Incorrect elevation assessment leads to drainage problems and code violations.

Evaluating warranty claims, determining root cause (construction defect vs homeowner damage vs normal wear), and scheduling repairs. Daniel specializes in this area. Requires knowledge of material lifecycles and construction methods.

Identify common quality issues by trade: framing (crown direction, nailing patterns, header sizes), plumbing (slope, venting, fitting types), electrical (box fill, wire gauge, GFCI placement), and roofing (shingle pattern, flashing, valleys).
OSHA requirements for residential construction sites: fall protection, trench safety, electrical safety, PPE requirements, housekeeping. Supervisors must enforce compliance and document violations.

daily_workflows.name: Daily Site Inspection Round
steps: Review previous day's logs and open issues
Check scheduled activities for today across all assigned houses
Plan optimized route based on priority and geography
Visit each house and verify sub crew is on site
Inspect work quality against FBC standards
Take photos of critical items and deficiencies
Write daily log for each house visited
Mark Checklist Done for completed activities (payment trigger)
Report urgent issues to Scheduling via WhatsApp
Upload photos and evidence to platform
critical_decisions: Whether work quality meets Checklist Done threshold
Whether to stop work and request correction before proceeding
Whether safety violation requires immediate work stoppage
Priority ranking of houses to visit given 50+ house load

daily_workflows.name: Checklist Done Verification
steps: Arrive at house with scheduled completion
Verify all scope items for the activity are complete
Check quality against standards checklist
Take completion photos
Mark Checklist Done in system (triggers H-01 payment)
Note any punch items that don't block completion
Update daily log with completion details
critical_decisions: Whether remaining items are punch-list (allow completion) or deficiency (block completion)
Whether to require sub to return before marking complete

daily_workflows.name: Engineering Loads Verification
steps: Review engineering loads calculation for house
Visit site to verify soil conditions match assumptions
Check footer dimensions against loads spec
Verify rebar size and spacing per loads document
Confirm fill depth and compaction requirements
Report loads verification to Scheduling (H-12)
critical_decisions: Whether site conditions match engineering assumptions
Whether additional fill or deeper footers are needed

daily_workflows.name: Monitor Daily Route Execution
steps: Receive route plan for the day (assigned houses)
Drive to each house in optimized order
Photograph overall progress at each house
Note sub crew presence and headcount
Identify any obvious safety or quality issues
Report findings to supervisor via WhatsApp
Enter basic log data in system
critical_decisions: Whether an issue is urgent enough to call supervisor immediately vs log for next day

daily_workflows.name: Repair Scheduling (Warranty)
steps: Receive warranty claim from customer service
Visit house to assess claim
Determine root cause (construction defect vs other)
If construction defect: identify responsible sub
Schedule repair with sub or handyman
Note: supervisors currently schedule repairs WITHOUT PO (pain point)
Verify repair completion
Update warranty claim status
critical_decisions: Whether claim is covered under warranty vs homeowner responsibility
Whether original sub is responsible for free repair vs new PO needed

handoffs.data_exchanged: Activity completion confirmation with date, house ID, sub ID, and completion photos. This triggers subcontractor payment processing.
handoffs.impact: If Checklist Done is premature, sub is paid for incomplete work. If delayed, sub cash flow suffers and relationship deteriorates. Most critical handoff in the system.

handoffs.data_exchanged: Deficiency description, photos, affected activity, estimated correction time, whether work must stop
handoffs.impact: Quality issues may require rescheduling downstream activities. If not communicated quickly, next trade may arrive to find site not ready.

handoffs.data_exchanged: House list with scheduled activities, assigned subs, expected completion dates
handoffs.impact: Without schedule information, supervisors cannot plan daily routes or know which houses to prioritize for verification

handoffs.data_exchanged: Engineering loads verification with foundation specifications, soil condition assessment, and go/no-go for foundation phase
handoffs.impact: Scheduling cannot proceed with foundation activities until loads are verified. This is a known bottleneck causing Phase 1 delays.

handoffs.data_exchanged: House ID, inspection type needed, activities completed, supervisor pre-check notes
handoffs.impact: If inspection is not requested promptly after completion, idle time between activities increases total phase duration

handoffs.data_exchanged: Failed items list, correction requirements, re-inspection timeline
handoffs.impact: Supervisor must coordinate correction with sub crew and verify fix before re-inspection can be scheduled

pain_points.description: Dashboards are read-only; supervisors cannot interact with data, drill down into details, or update status directly from the dashboard view
pain_points.current_workaround: Supervisors must navigate away from dashboard to individual records to make updates, losing context and wasting time
pain_points.proposed_solution: Interactive dashboards with inline editing, drill-down capability, and quick-action buttons for common operations (Checklist Done, log entry, issue flag)

pain_points.description: Photo upload to the platform fails 99% of the time; evidence documentation is essentially non-functional
pain_points.current_workaround: Photos stored on personal phones, shared via WhatsApp groups, or not documented at all. Critical pre-cover photos are lost.
pain_points.proposed_solution: Offline-first photo capture with background sync when connectivity is available; compressed upload with retry logic; local cache until confirmed upload

pain_points.description: 50+ houses per day is physically impossible to visit with proper inspection quality; monitors do drive-by assessments rather than thorough checks
pain_points.current_workaround: Prioritize houses with active pours, inspections, or reported issues; other houses get minimal or no coverage
pain_points.proposed_solution: Risk-based prioritization algorithm that identifies which houses need physical visits vs which can be verified via sub-reported photos; additional monitor hiring

pain_points.description: Supervisors schedule repairs directly with subs without creating a Purchase Order (PO), causing financial tracking gaps and unbudgeted expenses
pain_points.current_workaround: Repairs are scheduled informally via WhatsApp; costs appear as surprises in financial reconciliation
pain_points.proposed_solution: Quick PO creation workflow from mobile device; repair authorization limit per supervisor; auto-PO for warranty repairs below threshold amount

pain_points.description: No standardized quality checklists per activity type; quality assessment depends entirely on individual supervisor experience and judgment
pain_points.current_workaround: Experienced supervisors (JP, Daniel) maintain mental checklists; new monitors (Charles) have no reference standard
pain_points.proposed_solution: Digital quality checklists per activity type with FBC reference, photo requirements, and pass/fail criteria. Auto-populated when Checklist Done is attempted.

tribal_knowledge.description: JP Valenca maintains a mental model of which houses across all communities are 'problematic' - meaning they have recurring quality issues, difficult subs, drainage problems, or structural concerns. He adjusts his route to visit these houses more frequently without any system flag telling him to do so.

JP can assess site elevation and drainage adequacy by visual inspection and experience, without using surveying instruments. This includes knowing when fill is needed, estimating fill quantities, and predicting drainage problems from site topography. No documented methodology exists.

Experienced supervisors know which sub crews consistently have quality issues and what specific deficiencies to look for. For example, knowing that a specific framing crew tends to miss hurricane clips or that a plumbing crew doesn't slope drain lines adequately.

Daniel has extensive knowledge of which construction issues lead to warranty claims. He can predict warranty problems during construction by identifying early signs: insufficient attic ventilation leads to shingle failure, poor flashing leads to water intrusion, inadequate compaction leads to settlement cracks.

Luigi (experienced monitor) knows the optimal route sequences for each community, including which gates have access code changes, which construction entrances are seasonal, and time-of-day traffic patterns that affect travel time.

topics: What Checklist Done means in the payment pipeline
Quality verification steps before marking complete
Common mistakes that lead to premature payment
How to handle partial completion scenarios
How to read and interpret dashboard metrics
Custom view creation for daily priorities
FBC quality standards per activity type
Photo documentation requirements at each stage
Pass/fail criteria for each checklist item
When to stop work vs allow continuation
How to read engineering loads documents
Equipment needed for proper loads verification
Common warranty claim types and root causes
Repair scheduling workflow with PO requirement

compliance_requirements.requirement: Daily construction logs must be maintained for all active construction sites
compliance_requirements.verification: Audit daily log completeness weekly; flag houses with missing logs for more than 1 business day

compliance_requirements.requirement: Pre-cover photos required before any work is concealed (pre-pour, pre-drywall, pre-siding)
compliance_requirements.verification: Photo evidence must be timestamped and geotagged; verify photo exists before allowing Checklist Done for cover-up activities

compliance_requirements.requirement: OSHA fall protection required for all work above 6 feet
compliance_requirements.verification: Supervisor visual inspection during site visits; document compliance or violation in daily log

compliance_requirements.requirement: Trench safety requirements for excavations deeper than 5 feet
compliance_requirements.verification: Verify shoring or sloping before allowing workers to enter excavations; document in daily log

compliance_requirements.requirement: All subcontractor workers must have valid identification and authorization to work
compliance_requirements.verification: Periodic crew verification during site visits; document crew count in daily logs

compliance_requirements.requirement: Material storage must not block fire access routes or emergency exits
compliance_requirements.verification: Visual inspection during daily rounds; issue correction notice if violated

key_metrics.target: Supervisor: 15-20 thorough visits, Monitor: 30-40 check visits
key_metrics.measurement: Daily log entries count per person; GPS tracking of route coverage

key_metrics.target: <2% of Checklist Done items require rework or correction after marking
key_metrics.measurement: Track rework requests against houses where Checklist Done was marked; audit sample monthly

key_metrics.target: 100% of active houses have daily log entry on days with scheduled activity
key_metrics.measurement: Compare houses with scheduled activities vs houses with daily log entries for same day
Successful uploads divided by total upload attempts; track retry counts

key_metrics.target: Quality issues reported within 4 hours of discovery; safety issues immediately
key_metrics.measurement: Time between issue discovery (daily log timestamp) and notification to Scheduling/relevant department

key_metrics.target: <5 business days from claim to repair completion
key_metrics.measurement: Days between warranty claim receipt and repair completion verification
